title: Life Hacking for Educators
text:
Overview
Life hacks help us streamline our lives and make us feel clever—a not insignificant source of joy in these difficult times. As educators, many of us are invigorated by the positive energy we get from our students, and enervated by the drudgery of administrative tasks, often made more manual and more painful by opaque software systems. In this session, we celebrate our most clever and useful tricks for minimizing the headaches we incur from learning management systems and other academic edifices (e.g., Moodle, Canvas, Workday, etc.)
Session chair: Andrew Bray - Berkeley
Session organizer: Ben Baumer - Smith College

title: Life Hacking for Educators
text:
Homepage
All session materials can be viewed at https://beanumber.github.io/quarto-life-hacks-jsm26/. The GitHub repo that serves this site is at https://github.com/beanumber/quarto-life-hacks-jsm26/.

title: The LTS Document: Lab, Template and Solutions All in One
text: Assigning low-stakes practice problems or “lab” activities during class is one strategy for incorporating active learning into a traditional lecture-oriented course. In a course that incorporates computation, instructors may find it useful to provide a document with detailed information and instructions (a “lab” document), a document with initial scaffolding for students to work within (a “template” document), and a document with example results (a “solutions” document). However, keeping content synchronized across these three documents, as well as the learning management system used to distribute these documents, introduces administrative overhead and mistakes. This talk demonstrates how to use an LTS document (a custom document format for the Quarto publishing system) to create each of these student-facing files simultaneously from one main instructor-facing file, and automatically deploy them to Moodle using an R package.

title: The LTS Document: Lab, Template and Solutions All in One

title: Looking back and looking beyond
text:
Speaker

Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel is Professor of the Practice at Duke University and Developer Educator at Posit. Mine’s work focuses on innovation in statistics and data science pedagogy, with an emphasis on computing, reproducible research, student-centered learning, and open-source education as well as pedagogical approaches for enhancing retention of women and under-represented minorities in STEM. Mine works on the OpenIntro project, whose mission is to make educational products that are free, transparent, and lower barriers to education. As part of this project she co-authored four open-source introductory statistics textbooks – latest is the 2nd edition of Introduction to Modern Statistics. She is also a co-author on R for Data Science, the creator and maintainer of Data Science in a Box, and she teaches popular data analysis and data science with R courses on Coursera. Mine is a Fellow of the ASA and Elected Member of the ISI as well as a Waller and Hogg award winner for teaching excellence. In 2024, she was elected as Vice President of the International Association for Statistical Education (IASE).

title: gglm: A tool for teaching linear model diagnostics while adhering to the grammar of graphics
text: In this talk, I’ll discuss the gglm R package, which allows for easy creation of linear model diagnostic plots using the grammar of the graphics and ggplot2. More specifically, gglm provides a consistent and intuitive syntax to produce residual diagnostic plots for a variety of model types. We’ll cover the basics of the package, along with motivation and use cases for a modern statistics, statistical learning, or data science classroom. We’ll compare gglm’s diagnostic plot solution to solutions provided by base R and others. We’ll then discuss features of the package that allow for more robust analysis of residuals than typical model diagnostic tools, including different model types (i.e., engines) and multivariate diagnostics. Finally, I’ll showcase a shiny dashboard that allows the user to upload data, build a statistical model, and produce diagnostics powered by gglm, aimed at introductory statistics courses that don’t cover programming, but still want to use real data.

title: gglm: A tool for teaching linear model diagnostics while adhering to the grammar of graphics
text: In this talk, I’ll discuss the gglm R package, which allows for easy creation of linear model diagnostic plots using the grammar of the graphics and ggplot2. Speaker

Grayson White is a Visiting Assistant Professor of Statistics at Reed College. His research interests are in small area estimation, hierarchical Bayesian modeling, environmental statistics applied to forestry and natural resources problems, and statistical software development. More specifically, my research focuses on implementing model-based statistical methods for small area estimation of forest attributes, and developing methodologies for formal assessment of those estimators.

title: looking backand looking beyond
text:
use quarto

 Charlotte :: websites


Don’t start from scratch!
Tooling is there and well-documented for going from good to “stunning” websites, but it takes willingness to learn / get familiar with / copy-paste trial-and-error with web design tools – sound familiar?
There is no excuse for not having a personal (or otherwise) website made with Quarto

title: looking backand looking beyond
text: course websites + search

title: looking backand looking beyond
text:
student project websites



few .qmds
_quarto.yml



GitHub repo
GitHub action





.github/workflows/render-website.yaml

on:
  push:
    branches: main
  workflow_dispatch:

name: Render Website

jobs:
  build-website:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    container:
      image: ghcr.io/dukestatsci/r_gh_actions:latest
    env:
      GITHUB_PAT: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v2

      - name: Install packages
        run: |
          checklist::install_missing_pkgs()
        shell: Rscript {0}
        
      - name: Render Quarto Project
        run: |
          quarto render &&
          touch docs/.nojekyll
      
      - name: Deploy 🚀
        uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@v4
        with:
          clean: false
          branch: gh-pages
          folder: docs

title: looking backand looking beyond
text:
break quarto

 Colin :: parsermd / rundel.github.io/parsermd




A Quarto document is ultimately a document you can break apart
The document is structured in a way that quarto leverages that you can get at with other tooling as well
In a teaching context, breaking apart a Quarto document can enable automated section-level feedback or more…

title: looking backand looking beyond
section: a look back at breaking rmarkdown
text:
a look back at breaking rmarkdown

Make a template



gen-template.R

library(magrittr)
library(readr)

parsermd::parse_rmd("lab-06.Rmd") %>%
  parsermd::as_tibble() %>%
  dplyr::filter(sec_h3 != "Load packages" & !is.na(sec_h3)) %>%
  parsermd::rmd_template(keep_content = TRUE, keep_headings = TRUE) %>%
  write_rds(here::here(".github/workflows/template.rds"))

title: looking backand looking beyond
section: a look back at break rmarkdown
text:
a look back at break rmarkdown

Check against the a template with GitHub actions



.github/workflows/rmd_structure.yml

on:
  push:
    paths:
    - 'lab-06.Rmd'
name: Check Rmd Structure
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    container:
      image: ghcr.io/statprog-s1-2020/hw2-docker:latest
    steps:
    - name: Checkout
      uses: actions/checkout@master
    - name: Check Structure
      run: |
        tpl = readRDS(".github/workflows/template.rds")
        checklist::quit_on_failure({
          parsermd::rmd_check_template("lab-06.Rmd", tpl)
        })
      shell: Rscript {0}

title: Stop Repeating Yourself: Shared R Resources to Reduce Duplicated Prep
text: For introductory statistics courses that incorporate programming, a lot of time is spent creating and maintaining how-to guides and design class activities that introduce help students get up and running with the language, navigating the IDE, fundamental building blocks of the programming language, and practicing the commands to carryout analyses. This takes a lot of time and effort, which can be duplicated across faculty when there are many sections of the same course taught in a term. In this talk, we explore the use of an R “lab manual” paired with an R package that serves as a shared resource for the introductory statistics courses at Carleton College. We’ll discuss its usage, maintenance, and frustration-saving features. Along the way we’ll also discus a smattering of other “life hacks”, such as generating alternative text with AI and using quarto-live for class activities.

title: Automate all the things
text: Teaching modern data science and statistics courses presents numerous logistical challenges—from building and distributing assignments and other course materials to providing timely, consistent feedback. In this talk, we explore tools for automating the repetitive aspects of course administration, building on the core reproducible infrastructure of Git, GitHub, and Quarto. Examples will include the ghclass package, which streamlines classroom management on GitHub, enabling automated repository creation, assignment distribution, and collection. GitHub Actions, which extends this ecosystem with CI pipelines that automatically render documents, run checks, and provide feedback on student submissions. The parsermd and markrmd packages, which offer programmatic tools for parsing and manipulating R Markdown and Quarto documents, enabling both automated and more efficient manual assessment of student work. Finally, we look ahead to the emerging role of agentic AI tools in teaching, discussing both the opportunities and considerations for AI-driven automation in course management.

title: Automate all the things
text: Teaching modern data science and statistics courses presents numerous logistical challenges—from building and distributing assignments and other course materials to providing timely, consistent feedback. In this talk, we explore tools for automating the repetitive aspects of course administration, building on the core reproducible infrastructure of Git, GitHub, and Quarto. Examples will include the ghclass package, which streamlines classroom management on GitHub, enabling automated repository creation, assignment distribution, and collection. GitHub Actions, which extends this ecosystem with CI pipelines that automatically render documents, run checks, and provide feedback on student submissions. The parsermd and markrmd packages, which offer programmatic tools for parsing and manipulating R Markdown and Quarto documents, enabling both automated and more efficient manual assessment of student work. Finally, we look ahead to the emerging role of agentic AI tools in teaching, discussing both the opportunities and considerations for AI-driven automation in course management.

Speaker

Colin Rundel is Associate Professor of the Practice at Duke University where he has been teaching since 2012. His work focuses on teaching statistical computing to both undergraduate and graduate students in both R and Python.
